title: Background. In 1997, grapes and grape products such as wine gained the attention of the scientific community and the public as the result of a seminal publication describing the chemopreventive potential of resveratrol. Resveratrol is a phytoalexin found throughout nature, but the most common dietary source is the grape. At the time, the potential health benefits of consuming relatively high quantities of fruits and vegetables were well accepted, for example, five or more servings per day, but frankly, the grape was not as commonly recognized as some other foods, such as carrots and broccoli, in terms of health benefits. At least in part, these factors led the California Table Grape Commission to consider supporting basic and clinical research to explore the potential of grape consumption to impact health.

sentences.text: Background.

In 1997, grapes and grape products such as wine gained the attention of the scientific community and the public as the result of a seminal publication describing the chemopreventive potential of resveratrol.
Resveratrol is a phytoalexin found throughout nature, but the most common dietary source is the grape.

At the time, the potential health benefits of consuming relatively high quantities of fruits and vegetables were well accepted, for example, five or more servings per day, but frankly, the grape was not as commonly recognized as some other foods, such as carrots and broccoli, in terms of health benefits.
At least in part, these factors led the California Table Grape Commission to consider supporting basic and clinical research to explore the potential of grape consumption to impact health.
The California Table Grape Commission represents all table grape growers in the state, which produces 99 percent of the commercially grown table grapes in the USA.
It was established by an act of the state legislature in 1967 and approved every five years through grower referenda.

The purpose of the commission is to maintain and expand markets for fresh California grapes domestically and worldwide, and in doing so it engages in a variety of activities including research, education, marketing, and community service.
When it became clear that the commission had the will and resources to support grape research, one bedrock specification was established from the outset.
This was that the work would be investigator initiated, peer-reviewed, and of the highest scientific caliber.

Perhaps most importantly, the commission would follow the science, no matter what the outcome, and maintain a complete firewall in terms of influencing the design of the investigative work or interpreting the results.
That early decision has been honored for 25 years.
Thus, the question became how to proceed.
Naturally, it was necessary to first assess the state-of-the-art in terms of grape research.
For this purpose, it was decided to hold an international symposium.
The conference was held April 29 to May 1, 1998, in San Francisco and brought together the small group of scientists worldwide, who had conducted grape-related research to share their findings.
The meeting highlighted early work on resveratrol and other grape polyphenols; grapes and heart health; and grapes' potential against several different cancers.
The meeting closed with a round-table discussion about future grape research and what directions that could take.
A next important step was to assemble a small group of experts in the field to help flesh out the most effective way to organize the research program and the required administrative structure.
The most important outcomes were as follows.
First, there would be an open call for applications, worldwide, allowing investigators to propose what they thought would be of greatest interest.

Next, since funds were limited, the projects would receive seed grants, with the hope of supporting pilot studies that would generate sufficient data to leverage funding through more well-endowed organizations.

Of major importance, it was determined that a standardized product would need to be prepared under the auspices of the commission, and it was required that investigators would perform their work using this product.
Quite simply, the availability of this standardized product would ensure the scientific rigor of the work.
Others could reproduce the studies, and continuity could be assured year after year.
The preparation, characterization, and storage of the product have been described.

The first batch of standardized grape powder was produced in 1999, the first call for applications was released in 2000, the first peer review of the applications was performed in 2000, and the first seven projects were funded that same year.
Since that time, there have been 25 calls for applications, with 25 corresponding rounds of peer review, resulting in the funding or support of 95 studies.

The range of health benefits that have resulted and been revealed from these investigator-initiated proposals is truly astounding and beyond what might have been envisioned at the outset of the program.

There have been some milestone publications, including a Supplement published in the Journal of Nutrition, two Perspectives published in the Journal of Agriculture and Food Chemistry, the first edition of Grapes and Health, and the current edition of Grapes and Health.
These publications highlight many key discoveries, primarily in areas where studies have been performed in greater depth.
However, the coverage is not fully comprehensive.
In fact, there have been about 62 peer-reviewed publications resulting from work sponsored by the commission.
These publications are listed below, with a brief synopsis of key findings.
In sum, over the past 25 years, a great deal of knowledge regarding the health benefits of consuming grapes, in normal amounts between 1.5 and 2.5 cups per day, has been acquired.
Key areas with growing evidence include the heart, brain, skin, colon and gastrointestinal tract, and eyes.
In the process, grape growers have created an enduring model for industry-sponsored research.
Beyond a business perspective, the growing of grapes evokes passion.
It has always been obvious that the organoleptic properties of grapes are excellent.
The additional element of benefiting human health, to any extent whatsoever, adds to the passion.

The novelty of the scientific findings on table grapes generated as a result of the research inspired by this funding mechanism has taught us a great deal about the influence of diet and health, especially in regard to the importance of fruits and vegetables, in which the grape has served as a prototype.
